metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the docs AC — e.g. a pytest file-content assertion (`assert 'tombstone' in (repo_root/'docs/config.md').read_text()` and `assert 'rebar config validate' in ...`) or a CI lint step that verifies the required section headings are present.
evidence: AC text: 'docs/config.md — document the tombstone behaviour and the validate/doctor command.'
No described test, grep, or CI check asserts the file exists or contains any required section.
All other ACs name a specific test or CLI invocation; this one does not.
findings.finding: The docs acceptance criterion ('`docs/config.md` documents the tombstone behaviour and the new command') is effort-framed and has no proving command — no test or deterministic check verifies the file exists with the required content sections.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — last bullet: '`docs/config.md` documents the tombstone behaviour and the new command.'
scenarios: An executor ships the feature without updating docs and all other ACs pass — the gate does not catch it.
CI passes green with a missing or stale docs/config.md.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the docs AC: 'verified by `grep -q "tombstone" docs/config.md && grep -q "rebar config validate" docs/config.md` (run in the repo root as part of `make test` or a dedicated docs-lint step).'

findings.finding: The plan places the `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` tombstone check at the top of `load_config()`, but `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:_resolve_tracker_dir` (line 171-182) calls `tracker_dir_override()` directly — which only reads `os.environ.get('REBAR_TRACKER_DIR')` — and never calls `load_config()`. An operator running the reconciler with `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` set would bypass the tombstone entirely; the check needs to also fire at `tracker_dir_override()` or `tracker_dir()`, or the reconciler's direct call path must be enumerated as a required update site.
findings.location: What section / Scope (`src/rebar/config.py`) / `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py:171-182`
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` tombstone check into `tracker_dir_override()` itself (or at the top of `tracker_dir()`), not just `load_config()`. Since `tracker_dir_override()` is the single source for the env read, this covers all call paths — `load_config()`, `tracker_dir()`, and `_resolve_tracker_dir` — without additional per-site changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the AC6 grep pattern to use word-boundary anchors and cover tuple forms, e.g. `re.search(r'except\s+(Exception|BaseException)\b', line)` plus a separate pass for `except\s+\(.*\b(Exception|BaseException)\b', and document that the behavioral in-process test (not the grep) is the primary proof.
evidence: Pattern `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` matches `except ExceptionGroup:` (false positive — would require an unnecessary guard) and misses `except (Exception, ValueError):` (false negative — broad catch with no guard required).
Verify-command defect taxonomy item 1: substring false-positive; item 4: fixture may not exercise the actual claim.
The behavioral in-process test in AC6 covers the runtime behavior, but the grep is described as 'a reproducible check' for the guard's presence.
findings.finding: The source-grep pattern `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` used in AC6's pytest is a substring match with no word-boundary anchor and would both false-positive on `except ExceptionGroup` or `except ExceptionHandler` and false-negative on `except (Exception, SomeError):` multi-exception tuples — making the 'reproducible check' unreliable as specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC6: 'a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it'
scenarios: A future `except (Exception, SomeOther):` broad-catch in mcp_server.py is added — the grep misses it and the guard is absent, undetected.
A line containing `except ExceptionGroup:` triggers a spurious failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the grep-based check with an AST-walk over `mcp_server.py` that finds all `ExceptHandler` nodes whose `type` is or includes `Exception`/`BaseException` and asserts each has a preceding sibling that re-raises `RemovedInputError` — more robust than regex on source text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `pytest-socket` as the primary mechanism in AC2 with `unittest.mock.patch` (which is already available), or add `pytest-socket` to dev dependencies explicitly — either way make the AC unambiguous about which mechanism is authoritative.
evidence: pytest-socket absent from pyproject.toml [project.optional-dependencies] dev section (lines 147-181)
No pytest-socket hits in any search across the repo
Plan text: 'enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch`…' — pytest-socket is the named first option but is uninstalled
Evidence class: Contradicted — the environment (dev deps) falsifies pytest-socket availability as a primary mechanism
findings.finding: `pytest-socket` is referenced in AC2 as the primary network-blocking mechanism for test assertions, but it is not installed in the project's dev dependencies (pyproject.toml lists pytest, pytest-cov, pytest-xdist, pytest-split — no pytest-socket). While the plan offers `unittest.mock.patch` as a fallback in the same sentence, the AC phrasing 'enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch`' leaves `pytest-socket` as the leading option, risking a contributor adding a `pytest.mark.socket_disabled` decorator that silently has no effect.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC line 2: 'no network call (enforced via `pytest-socket`, or a `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive asserting `call_count == 0`)'
scenarios: A contributor follows the AC literally and reaches for pytest-socket first, discovers it is not installed, then either adds it to dev deps (unreviewed new dep) or falls back to mock.patch without updating the AC.
CI runs the test without pytest-socket installed; a `@pytest.mark.disable_socket` mark is silently ignored (it is a no-op without the plugin), so the network-blocking assertion passes vacuously.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the AC2 network-isolation clause to: 'no network call (enforced by `unittest.mock.patch` on the lowest-level HTTP/socket primitive, asserting `call_count == 0`)' — removing the pytest-socket alternative to avoid the vacuous-no-op risk, since mock.patch is already available with zero new dependencies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the MCP write-path AC test vehicle: `create_ticket` is a nested closure, not an importable symbol — specify instead `rebar.create_ticket()` (the public library function that the MCP tool delegates to) or a FastMCP test-client invocation pattern, whichever correctly exercises the config-load → tombstone-raise path.
evidence: _mcp_writes.py:48 shows `def create_ticket(...)` is defined as a nested function INSIDE `register_write_tools()` decorated with `@mcp.tool(...)`
There is no module-level `create_ticket` symbol in either mcp_server.py or _mcp_writes.py
Search for `def create_ticket` returns no module-level definition
findings.finding: The plan's AC says to verify the MCP write path by calling 'an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)'. In the actual codebase, `create_ticket` is a nested closure defined inside `register_write_tools()` and registered on the FastMCP server object — it is not a module-level importable symbol in `_mcp_writes.py` or `mcp_server.py`. There is no `rebar.mcp_server.create_ticket` or `rebar._mcp_writes.create_ticket` to call directly in a test.
findings.location: AC: 'MCP-server exit semantics' bullet — 'verified by an in-process test that ... calls an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)'
scenarios: Implementer writes `from rebar._mcp_writes import create_ticket` and gets ImportError, blocking the test.
Implementer must instead call `rebar.create_ticket()` (the library function) or invoke the tool via the FastMCP test client, neither of which is what the plan specifies.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'calls an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)' with 'calls `rebar.create_ticket(...)` directly (the library function the MCP tool delegates to) after priming the config cache and setting the retired env var, and asserts `RemovedInputError` propagates'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify (and enforce via an AC) that the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone entry is registered in the central `_deprecations.py` registry so `scan_tombstones` finds it, even if the *raising* path is applied in `LLMConfig.from_env()`; add a validate-command test asserting `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` appears in its output.
evidence: '`REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`→`REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` lives in the optional `rebar.llm` layer (not `config.py`/`_config_schema.py`), so its tombstone is applied in the LLM config load path'
'scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths) -> list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]] that walks the registry and returns all matches'

No plan text clarifies that the LLM tombstone is also registered in `_deprecations.py` so `scan_tombstones` can find it; the phrasing 'applied in the LLM config load path' suggests a local check, not a central registration.
findings.finding: The `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone is described as 'applied in the LLM config load path' (`src/rebar/llm/`) rather than registered in the central `_deprecations.py` registry, but `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths)` is described as walking 'the registry' — leaving it ambiguous whether `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` would detect a set `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`. If the LLM tombstone is only a local check in `LLMConfig.from_env()` and not in `_deprecations.py`, `scan_tombstones` silently misses it, and the validate/doctor command gives a false-all-clear to operators with that var set.
findings.location: Scope section: '`src/rebar/llm/` (`LLMConfig.from_env()`) — apply the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone there (it is not a core-`config.py` var)'; AC: '`rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` uses `scan_tombstones(...)` to walk the registry'
scenarios: `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` is set in a deployment; operator runs `rebar config validate` which reports no issues; but when an LLM command runs, the tombstone fires (or silently ignores if only a raise path was added); validate gave a false-all-clear.

The AC for `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` is a dedicated test in `rebar.llm` that verifies the tombstone fires via `LLMConfig.from_env()`, but no AC verifies it appears in `scan_tombstones` output — the validate path is untested for this input.
findings.suggested_fix: Register all tombstones — including `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` — in `_deprecations.py`. In `LLMConfig.from_env()`, call the shared `check_tombstones(env)` helper (same one `load_config()` calls) rather than a local check, so the central registry is the single source of truth for both the raising path and `scan_tombstones`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the scope of the `except RemovedInputError: raise` guard audit: explicitly list whether `_mcp_writes.py:288/322/327` (background workflow `except Exception` blocks) also need guards, or confirm they are exempt because config load does not occur inside them — the AC grep test as written would flag them.
evidence: Plan: 'the except Exception blocks at mcp_server.py:302-312/:311 are updated to except RemovedInputError: raise'
Grep of mcp_server.py shows exactly ONE except Exception at line 311 (not two blocks)
_mcp_writes.py lines 288, 322, 327 each have `except Exception` but wrap background workflow execution, not config load
Plan AC: 'every line matching except\s+(Exception|BaseException) has a RemovedInputError: raise guard immediately above it' — this would catch _mcp_writes.py blocks too
findings.finding: The plan claims the MCP boot path has `except Exception` blocks (plural, 'mcp_server.py:302-312 and :311') implying two separate handlers to guard, but there is only ONE except Exception block in mcp_server.py's boot path (line 311). However, `_mcp_writes.py` has three additional `except Exception` blocks (lines 288, 322, 327) that the plan does not enumerate in its AC grep/re-raise scope. The plan's AC says to add re-raise guards to 'mcp_server.py:302-312/:311' but does not specify whether _mcp_writes.py blocks also need guards — those wrap background thread execution, not config load, so they are less critical, but the plan's stated audit ('grep the config→CLI→MCP path… confirm no bare except: / except BaseException swallows it') would surface them without directing what to do.
findings.location: What section — 'Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers' bullet; AC line 'confirmed to re-raise it'
scenarios: Implementer adds the re-raise guard only to mcp_server.py:311 (the only one the plan names), then the AC pytest that greps ALL `except Exception` lines fails due to the _mcp_writes.py ones — unless the AC is interpreted to exempt those.
Plan's AC grep check is broader than the plan's re-raise prescription, creating an inconsistency the implementer must resolve.
findings.suggested_fix: Narrow the AC grep to `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and `src/rebar/config.py` (the config→boot path), or add an explicit exemption comment pattern (e.g. `# noqa: tombstone-exempt`) that the pytest can recognize, so the audit does not require adding re-raise guards to unrelated workflow exception handlers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `str` element of `scan_tombstones`'s return tuple — e.g., specify it as the matched raw value (the env var value or TOML key string that triggered the match), and document this in the function's docstring and the Scope section.
evidence: Plan defines 'scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths) -> list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]] that walks the registry and returns all matches without raising'
The AC states the command 'prints every tombstone/alias with old-name + replacement + removed-in' — requiring the consumer to know what the str carries
No definition of the str element appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths) -> list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]]` return-tuple's second element (`str`) is never defined — its meaning (matched value? formatted message? source label?) is unspecified. The `config validate`/`doctor` command must consume this to 'print every tombstone/alias with old-name + replacement + removed-in', so the display logic cannot be implemented without inventing a convention the plan never states.
findings.location: What section — 'scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths) -> list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]]'; also AC: 'rebar config validate / rebar doctor'
scenarios: An implementer writing the `config validate` output formatter has no spec for what `tuple[1]` contains — they must invent a convention, risking a mismatch between the scan function and the CLI consumer.
A test asserting the scan return value has no spec to assert against.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the return type to `list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]]` where `str` is the matched raw input (env var value or TOML dotted-key string), documented explicitly; or introduce a small `TombstoneMatch` named tuple/dataclass with `tombstone: RemovedInput`, `source: str` (e.g. `"env:TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR"`), and `raw_value: str` to make the display contract self-documenting.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the warn-class tombstone signal path explicitly — either name a new `warn_tombstone(t: RemovedInput)` helper in `_deprecations.py` that emits `logging.warning` directly (bypassing `REGISTRY`), or clarify that warn-class tombstones call `logger.warning(...)` directly without routing through `warn_deprecated`.
evidence: Plan: 'the warning is emitted via the existing deprecation-warning path (logging.warning on the rebar logger, the same channel warn_deprecated uses)'
_deprecations.py:132: `dep = REGISTRY[key]  # KeyError on an unregistered surface — intentional (completeness)`
RemovedInput entries are explicitly kept separate from REGISTRY: 'These are separate from the existing alias-registry _KINDS … do not overload it'
No new warn_tombstone() or equivalent function is named in the plan
findings.finding: The warn-class tombstone signal path is described as 'the existing deprecation-warning path (logging.warning on the rebar logger, the same channel warn_deprecated uses)' but `warn_deprecated()` (the named channel) requires a key in `REGISTRY` (the `Deprecation` alias registry) and raises `KeyError` on miss — tombstone entries live in a separate registry and are `RemovedInput` objects, not `Deprecation` objects. No bridging helper, alternate call, or adaptation is specified, leaving implementers to either add tombstone entries to the wrong registry or invent an undocumented `warn_tombstone()` function.
findings.location: What section — 'emitted via the existing deprecation-warning path (logging.warning on the rebar logger, the same channel warn_deprecated uses)'; Scope: src/rebar/_deprecations.py
scenarios: An implementer calling warn_deprecated('env:TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR') for a warn-class tombstone gets a KeyError because that key is not in REGISTRY.
An implementer who adds tombstone entries to REGISTRY to avoid KeyError conflates the two registries the plan explicitly separates, breaking the registry completeness test.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope/_deprecations.py section: 'Add a `warn_tombstone(t: RemovedInput, *, logger) -> None` helper that emits `logger.warning(...)` directly (not via `warn_deprecated`, which requires a REGISTRY entry) — the tombstone registry is the completeness check for this path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact placement and conditionality of the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone check in `LLMConfig.from_env()`: state whether it fires unconditionally (regardless of whether `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` is also set) or only when `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` is unset, and whether it fires at the top of `from_env()` before the `_llm_int` calls.
evidence: Plan: 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS lives in the optional rebar.llm layer (not config.py/_config_schema.py), so its tombstone is applied in the LLM config load path (see Scope), classified warn'
LLMConfig.from_env() at llm/config.py:601 calls _llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS', 'max_steps', DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS) — no existing tombstone hook
Plan does not name a specific line, function, or before/after rule for the placement
No description of what happens when both REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS and REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS are set simultaneously
findings.finding: The placement of the `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone check within `LLMConfig.from_env()` is unspecified. The plan says it is 'applied in the LLM config load path (see Scope)' and classified warn, but `LLMConfig.from_env()` resolves env vars via `_llm_int(table, cli, 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS', 'max_steps', ...)` — there is no described hook where the check would fire (before the `_llm_int` call? as an extra probe at the top of `from_env()`? inside a new wrapper?). Without a placement rule, an implementer cannot determine whether the tombstone fires before or after the default is applied, or whether it fires at all when `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` is also set.
findings.location: What section — 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS→REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS lives in the optional rebar.llm layer … its tombstone is applied in the LLM config load path'; Scope: 'src/rebar/llm/ (LLMConfig.from_env())'
scenarios: Implementer adds the check at the top of from_env() so it fires even when REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS is also set, causing a warn even for operators who have already migrated but haven't cleaned up the old var.

Implementer adds it only when REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS is unset (mirroring the old alias-only-when-canonical-unset pattern), but the plan doesn't say this — the difference matters for the test asserting 'REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS set and calling LLMConfig.from_env() surfaces the tombstone'.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the env-var tombstone pattern from config.py: check `os.environ.get('REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS')` at the TOP of `LLMConfig.from_env()` unconditionally (regardless of REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS) and emit `warn_tombstone(...)`, then proceed — the old var's value is never used.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `if argv and argv[0] == 'doctor':` intercept in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:main()` (and list `_cli/__init__.py` in the Scope) so `rebar doctor` dispatches to the `scan_tombstones`-based validate path instead of falling through to 'unknown subcommand'.
evidence: Plan Scope says 'New CLI subcommand (config validate / doctor) + wiring (show_config.py)' — does not name _cli/__init__.py for a doctor intercept.
Existing _cli/__init__.py:490-493 handles 'config' → show_config.config_cli(argv[1:]) but has no 'doctor' arm.
All known subcommands must have a registered intercept or dispatch arm in _cli/__init__.py main() to avoid the 'unknown subcommand' rejection at line 516-519.
findings.finding: `rebar doctor` named as a top-level command but `_cli/__init__.py` is not listed in Scope for adding a `doctor` dispatch intercept. The existing `config` intercept routes `rebar config [...]` → `show_config.config_cli(argv[1:])`, but `rebar doctor` would need its own `if argv and argv[0] == 'doctor':` block in `main()`. Without this wiring, `rebar doctor` would fall through to 'unknown subcommand' error.
findings.location: Scope section, `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:main()` / What section ('rebar config validate / rebar doctor')
scenarios: An operator runs `rebar doctor` and receives 'Error: unknown subcommand doctor' because no intercept was added to main().
The AC test for `rebar config validate` passes but `rebar doctor` is never wired.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `_cli/__init__.py` Scope entry: 'add a `doctor` intercept aliasing `config validate`'; insert `if argv and argv[0] == 'doctor': return show_config.validate_cli(argv[1:])` in `main()` alongside the `config` intercept.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is initially generated (e.g. `pytest --generate-golden` flag, a Makefile target, or a one-time script) and add that step to the implementation sequencing or Scope.
evidence: Directory listing of tests/golden/ shows only cli_help/ — config_surface.json is absent.
The plan says the pytest 'diffs against a committed `tests/golden/config_surface.json`' but gives no mechanism for its initial creation.
Without the seed file the CI test would always fail (file-not-found) until it is manually bootstrapped.
findings.finding: `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is named as a committed artifact the anti-regression CI test reads, but it does not currently exist in the repo (`tests/golden/` contains only `cli_help/`). The plan never specifies a bootstrap command or generation script to create the initial file.
findings.location: Scope section: '`tests/golden/config_surface.json` (committed) + a pytest that … diffs against the golden file'
scenarios: CI is immediately red after the file is committed because the golden file was never generated.
A developer doesn't know whether to run `pytest --update-golden`, a dedicated script, or generate it by hand.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly scope `docs/config.md` work to include reviewing and updating (not only appending) any existing content describing the warn/ignore / `REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS` behavior for retired keys, so the post-change docs accurately distinguish genuinely-unknown keys (keep old policy) from tombstoned keys (new fail-loud policy).
evidence: Scope says: 'docs/config.md — document the tombstone behaviour and the new command' — additive framing only, no mention of reviewing existing content
The plan explicitly changes behavior: 'removed TOML keys get only the generic _config_schema.py unknown key … (typo?) warning-and-ignore' → now raise RemovedInputError
If docs/config.md currently describes the WARN/REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error behavior for retired keys, that section would be stale after this change
findings.finding: The plan specifies adding tombstone behavior to `docs/config.md` but does not explicitly call for reviewing or updating existing docs/config.md content that may describe the prior forward-compat behavior (WARN/ignore for retired keys under `REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS`). Since the plan changes the behavior for a specific class of keys from silent-ignore to fail-loud, existing documentation of that behavior would become stale and contradictory.
findings.location: Scope section — 'docs/config.md'; AC — last bullet 'docs/config.md documents the tombstone behaviour and the new command'
scenarios: An operator reads the existing docs/config.md section on unknown/retired key handling, follows the old guidance (set REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error), and is surprised when a tombstoned key raises RemovedInputError regardless of that env var
A future contributor sees the docs' description of the warn/ignore policy and adds a new removed key using that path instead of the tombstone registry
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope entry for docs/config.md: 'Review existing forward-compat / unknown-key documentation and update to distinguish genuinely-unknown keys (WARN / REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error unchanged) from tombstoned retired keys (RemovedInputError / warn regardless of REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a persistent rationale for `RemovedInputError(BaseException)` in a committed artifact — either a module-level docstring in `_deprecations.py` (e.g., `# DESIGN: BaseException, not Exception — see docs/adr/tombstone-base-exception.md`) or a new ADR entry — so the constraint survives beyond this plan and resists future 'cleanup' refactors.
evidence: 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type — class RemovedInputError(BaseException). A RemovedInput... raises a dedicated RemovedInputError that subclasses BaseException (NOT Exception, NOT ConfigError). This is the load-bearing choice, not stylistic'
Plan lists docs/config.md as the only doc target — no ADR, no CLAUDE.md, no inline module docstring requirement
A docs/config.md entry covers user-facing behavior but would not explain the internal exception-hierarchy constraint to a future contributor
findings.finding: The plan introduces `class RemovedInputError(BaseException)` — an architecturally non-obvious choice where the entire security guarantee rests on BaseException NOT being catchable by the existing `except Exception` and `except ConfigError` handlers. This rationale is explained only inline in the plan document itself, not in any committed artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or inline module docstring). A future maintainer seeing `RemovedInputError(BaseException)` without context would likely 'fix' it to `Exception` or `ConfigError`, silently re-creating the exact silent-swallow vulnerability the plan prevents.
findings.location: What section — 'Distinct, un-swallowable error type' bullet; Scope — src/rebar/_deprecations.py
scenarios: A future contributor refactors RemovedInputError to subclass ConfigError or Exception (a seemingly 'cleaner' design), unknowingly re-enabling the silent-swallow at mcp_server.py:302-312 and tracker_dir()'s except ConfigError
A code reviewer requests the change to Exception as a 'best practice' and the author cannot cite a committed rationale
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring or comment block to `class RemovedInputError(BaseException)` in `_deprecations.py` explaining: (1) why BaseException and not Exception, (2) which specific except-clauses it bypasses, and (3) what the consequence of changing it would be. Optionally create `docs/adr/NNN-tombstone-base-exception.md`. Also add a lint/test guard: a pytest that asserts `issubclass(RemovedInputError, BaseException) and not issubclass(RemovedInputError, Exception)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that warn-class tombstone messages are ALSO printed to `sys.stderr` (or the CLI's warning channel) unconditionally during CLI and MCP startup — not only emitted to the `rebar` logger — so operators see them regardless of logging configuration; update the AC to assert stderr output, not just `caplog`.
evidence: 'the warning is emitted via the existing deprecation-warning path (`logging.warning` on the `rebar` logger, the same channel `warn_deprecated` uses)'
AC verifies warn-class via `caplog` capture — a test-only mechanism that says nothing about whether the user sees output in production
No plan text specifies a minimum stderr print for warn-class tombstones during normal CLI or MCP execution
findings.finding: Warn-class tombstone output during normal CLI/MCP operation relies solely on `logging.warning` on the `rebar` logger, which may be invisible to operators if the logger is not configured to emit to stderr (the typical library `NullHandler` default). An operator with a stale warn-class input (e.g. `REBAR_PUSH`, `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES`) would see no output whatsoever during normal command execution, defeating the 'fail-loud' goal for that class.
findings.location: What section (warn-only behavior) + AC: 'Operationally-inert retired inputs … warn rather than error — the warning is emitted via the existing deprecation-warning path (`logging.warning` on the `rebar` logger)'
scenarios: Operator upgrades with `REBAR_RECONCILER_LOCK_MAX_RETRIES` still set in their environment; runs `rebar reconcile`; logger has NullHandler; no output; operator never learns the var is ignored.
CI/CD pipeline sets stale warn-class env var; pipeline passes cleanly; operator believes configuration is active.
findings.suggested_fix: In the warn-class tombstone handler, emit a `sys.stderr.write(f"WARNING: {old_name} is removed (use {replacement}, removed in {removed_in})\n")` before or alongside the `logging.warning` call, mirroring what many CLIs do (e.g. pip's deprecation warnings always go to stderr). Update the AC to assert the warning appears on stderr (e.g. via `capsys`) in addition to the log record.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the source-grep AC ('a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)`…') and replace it with an expanded behavioral contract in the existing in-process AC6 test: verify that if ANY except-block on the full config→MCP call chain catches the exception, the RemovedInputError still propagates to the test boundary (e.g., assert it is not caught by adding a second handler in the test harness that would detect a swallowed re-raise).
evidence: 'a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it (a reproducible check, not a hand grep)'
This test verifies text structure, not runtime behavior — it is the source-grep-as-behavioral-test anti-pattern explicitly called out by E5 as MAJOR (locks in the bug / code style).

The plan already has a correct behavioral test for the same guarantee (in-process call asserting RemovedInputError propagates), making the grep test an additional oracle risk without coverage benefit.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` source text and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it. This is a source-grep masquerading as a behavioral test: it checks code structure/text rather than runtime behavior. It would pass if the guard is present syntactically but placed incorrectly (e.g., after a logger call that consumes the exception in practice), fail spuriously after a valid refactor that uses a helper or changes indentation, and would not detect a handler added elsewhere in the call chain that swallows the error. The plan already includes a correct in-process behavioral test for this guarantee (AC6: call the write-tool handler and assert `RemovedInputError` propagates), making the grep test redundant but risky — it locks in a particular code-structure style as a test oracle.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (MCP server path), paragraph: 'Plus a pytest that reads `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it'
scenarios: A future refactor extracts the re-raise into a context manager or decorator — the grep test fails falsely, blocking CI, despite correct behavior.
The guard is present syntactically but a surrounding logger call swallows the exception first — the grep passes while the behavioral guarantee is broken.
A new except-block is added in a different module on the config→MCP call path; the grep (scoped to mcp_server.py) passes while the new block silently swallows RemovedInputError.
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the grep-based AC. Expand AC6 to: (a) confirm `RemovedInputError` propagates through the full in-process call (already present), (b) add a negative test that temporarily installs a broad `except Exception: pass` mock at the request-dispatch boundary and confirms the test harness detects it (so the test suite would have caught the original bug the plan is fixing), and (c) add a comment in the AC noting that the behavioral test is the source of truth and structural review is a code-review concern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the new `tests/golden/config_surface.json` anti-regression guard can be implemented by extending the existing `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` + `docs/env-vars.md` drift gate (e.g. adding a tombstone-completeness check to `gen_env_registry.py`) rather than introducing a second, parallel golden-file mechanism.
evidence: Existing drift gate: tests/unit/test_gen_env_registry.py::test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree asserts gen.main(['--check']) == 0 — same commit-golden-diff pattern
scripts/gen_env_registry.py already scans the live key/alias sets and compares against docs/env-vars.md

Plan proposes a NEW tests/golden/config_surface.json + pytest that 'imports the live allowed-key set (_config_schema) + env vars and the tombstone/alias registries (_deprecations.py), diffs against a committed ... JSON'
The two mechanisms use the same pattern (generate → commit → CI diff) applied to overlapping domains (env-var surface vs. config-key surface)
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new `tests/golden/config_surface.json` + dedicated pytest diff as the anti-regression guard for removed keys lacking tombstone entries. The codebase already has a structurally identical drift-gate mechanism: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` generates `docs/env-vars.md` from the live key/alias sets, and `tests/unit/test_gen_env_registry.py::test_check_mode_clean_against_committed_tree` asserts the committed file matches generator output. The proposed golden-file mechanism is a second, parallel instance of the same pattern rather than extending the existing one.
findings.location: Plan section 'Prevent future silent removals (anti-regression guard)' / Scope bullet 'tests/golden/config_surface.json' / AC bullet 'A CI/anti-regression check fails...'
scenarios: Two drift-gate mechanisms covering overlapping surfaces will diverge over time, require dual-maintenance, and confuse contributors about which one to update when adding a key.
A contributor adding a new config key may update one golden file but not the other, causing a spurious CI failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `gen_env_registry.py` (or its test in `test_gen_env_registry.py`) to additionally verify that every key present in the previous golden output but absent from the current live set has a corresponding entry in `_deprecations.TOMBSTONE_REGISTRY`, then fail `--check` mode if not. This reuses the existing mechanism rather than introducing a parallel one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to the leaf plan (and a corresponding comment on the ticket before Gerrit votes) recording an Accept/Reject disposition with one-line rationale for each of the 20 advisory plan-review findings, and reflect every Accepted finding in the leaf description — as required by the parent epic's AC before LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes are cast.
evidence: Parent AC: 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale (as a comment on the child ticket), and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes.'
Leaf attestation manifest: 'advisory: 20' — 20 advisory findings exist but no Accept/Reject comments are recorded on the child ticket.
Leaf ACs list 10 items; none references the parent-mandated disposition comment requirement.
findings.finding: The parent epic's Acceptance Criteria impose a mandatory process gate on every child story: 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition and a one-line rationale (as a comment on the child ticket), and every Accepted finding is reflected in the child's description before that child's change receives its Gerrit LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes.' The leaf plan's own ACs make no mention of this parent-level requirement, and the leaf ticket currently carries zero comments — meaning the advisory findings (20 advisory findings per the attestation manifest) have no recorded dispositions. Under the parent's AC, Gerrit votes cannot be cast until dispositions are present; the leaf plan omits this gate entirely, creating a gap between the parent contract and what the leaf treats as done.
findings.location: Parent epic AC: 'Each child story's plan-review advisory findings are recorded with an explicit Accept/Reject disposition...' / Leaf ticket: `"comments": []` / Leaf ACs (no mention of disposition gate)
scenarios: The leaf's Gerrit change receives its LLM-Review +1 / Verified +1 votes without the 20 advisory findings ever being triaged, violating the parent's AC — which the parent treats as a precondition for closure of the entire epic.

A future audit of the child ticket finds no trace of which advisory findings were accepted and reflected in the description, making the parent's 'every Accepted finding is reflected' requirement unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a final AC to the leaf: '[ ] Before the Gerrit change receives LLM-Review +1 or Verified +1 votes, all plan-review advisory findings (currently 20) are recorded on this ticket as a comment with explicit Accept/Reject disposition and one-line rationale; every Accepted finding is reflected in this description.' Then triage the 20 advisories now and post the dispositions as a ticket comment.

findings.finding: The plan specifies only single-input test cases for `rebar config validate` / `scan_tombstones()`; no test verifies the critical ALL-matches behavior when multiple tombstoned inputs are simultaneously present — the key design differentiator over the raising `load_config()` path.
findings.location: AC: `rebar config validate` / `rebar doctor` bullet; Scope: `scan_tombstones(env, toml_tables, file_paths) -> list[tuple[RemovedInput, str]]`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `rebar config validate` AC: 'When TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR and verify.require_verdict_for_close are both set, `rebar config validate` exits non-zero and the output contains both the TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR and verify.require_verdict_for_close tombstone messages (verifying scan_tombstones does not abort on the first match).' Mirror this with a pytest parametrize or fixture that sets multiple inputs and asserts len(results) >= 2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2) with a test that primes `_RESULT_CACHE` with a clean config, then mutates the config file to add a tombstoned TOML key (e.g. `verify.require_verdict_for_close`), calls `load_config()` again, and asserts `RemovedInputError` is raised — proving the file-change cache-invalidation → re-resolve → TOML-tombstone path end-to-end.
evidence: AC1 text: 'verified by a test that sets a retired env var, primes the cache with a prior `load_config()`, and confirms the tombstone still fires' — tests only env-var tombstone on warm cache.

No AC describes a test that (a) primes the cache with a clean TOML config, (b) mutates the config file to add a tombstoned key, (c) calls `load_config()` again, and (d) asserts `RemovedInputError` is raised (proving the file-change cache-invalidation → re-resolve → TOML tombstone path).
If the 'inherently warm-cache-safe' invariant is ever violated, TOML tombstones silently stop working on warm-cache hits.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the TOML-key tombstone in a cache-related scenario, leaving the 'inherently warm-cache-safe' invariant for TOML tombstones unverified. The plan relies on the invariant that `_resolve()` only runs on a cache miss and raises before populating `_RESULT_CACHE`, but no test validates this invariant or the scenario where a config-file change triggers a cache miss → re-resolve → TOML tombstone fires.
findings.location: What section — 'TOML-key tombstones are checked inside `_resolve()` … inherently warm-cache-safe … by the existing invariant'; Acceptance Criteria AC1 (covers env-var warm-cache only).
scenarios: A future refactor widens the warm-cache path to skip `_resolve()` even on file changes — TOML tombstones silently stop firing, undetected because no test covers this path.
An executor misplaces the TOML check outside `_resolve()` and the invariant argument no longer applies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected JSON schema for `tests/golden/config_surface.json` in the plan — e.g. `{"env_vars": ["REBAR_TRACKER_DIR", ...], "toml_keys": ["verify.require_completion_verification_for_close", ...]}` with a note on whether aliases and tombstones are included or excluded from the live set.
evidence: The plan states the pytest 'diffs against a committed `tests/golden/config_surface.json`' but does not specify the file's schema (e.g. `{"env_vars": [...], "toml_keys": [...]}` vs a flat list vs a dict of kind→names).
The diff logic ('fails if a key is in neither the live set nor a tombstone/alias') implies a specific structure, but the executor must infer it.
This is a defaultable gap — the executor will choose a schema — but the choice affects what the CI check actually validates.
findings.finding: The format/schema of `tests/golden/config_surface.json` is not specified — an executor must independently decide what keys the file contains, how env-var names vs. TOML keys are represented, and what a 'removed without tombstone' diff looks like — making the CI golden-file anti-regression check underspecified.
findings.location: Scope bullet: '`tests/golden/config_surface.json` (committed) + a pytest that imports the live allowed-key set … diffs against the golden file'
scenarios: Two executors independently implement the golden file with incompatible schemas, causing confusion on the first update.
The chosen schema omits env-var aliases vs. tombstones as distinct entries, weakening the anti-regression guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line schema example to the Scope bullet: e.g. `{"allowed_env": [...sorted list of currently-valid env var names...], "allowed_toml": [...sorted list of currently-valid TOML keys...]}` — the pytest compares live `_config_schema` + `_deprecations` outputs against these lists and fails on any deletion not matched by a tombstone entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the TOML-tombstone detection site contradiction: explicitly state whether `_config_schema.py` or `_resolve()` owns the `RemovedInputError` raise for retired TOML keys, and describe how the two modules interact (e.g., schema raises directly, or schema flags the key and _resolve() raises on the flag).
evidence: "TOML-key tombstones (`verify.require_verdict_for_close`, `reconciler.*`) are checked **inside `_resolve()`** (where the TOML is already parsed) and raise there"
"`src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — route a known retired **TOML** key (`verify.require_verdict_for_close`, `reconciler.*`) to a targeted removed-key error rather than the generic 'typo?' warning."
findings.finding: The plan names two different modules as the site of the TOML-key tombstone check for the same keys (`verify.require_verdict_for_close`, `reconciler.*`): the What section says they are 'checked inside `_resolve()` (where the TOML is already parsed) and raise there,' while the Scope section says `_config_schema.py` should 'route a known retired TOML key… to a targeted removed-key error rather than the generic typo? warning.' An implementer cannot tell from the plan whether `_config_schema.py` or `_resolve()` owns the detection and raise logic — both cannot own it without explicit coordination described.
findings.location: What section ('TOML-key tombstones … are checked inside `_resolve()`') vs. Scope section ('`src/rebar/_config_schema.py` — route a known retired TOML key … to a targeted removed-key error')
scenarios: Implementer puts check in _resolve() and leaves _config_schema.py unchanged — schema still emits the generic 'typo?' warning first, confusing users.

Implementer puts check in _config_schema.py — but _resolve() is described as the check site, leaving _resolve() with dead check code or no check at all, and the warm-cache-safety argument (which relies on _resolve() raising before populating the cache) may break.
findings.suggested_fix: Pick one site: since the warm-cache-safety argument in What depends on `_resolve()` raising *before* populating `_RESULT_CACHE`, have `_resolve()` own the raise (walk the TOML tombstone registry after parsing but before writing to cache). Update the Scope bullet for `_config_schema.py` to say it no longer emits the generic 'typo?' warning for tombstoned keys (suppress that path) so there's no double-signal — and clarify that `_config_schema.py`'s role is suppression, not detection.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `RemovedInputError(BaseException)` hard-error behavior: describe either (a) an expand-contract sequence (warn-only release first, then error in a subsequent release) or (b) a documented emergency override mechanism (e.g., `REBAR_TOMBSTONE_OVERRIDE=1` for a single grace release), and state how library consumers of `load_config()` should handle the new `BaseException` subclass.
evidence: "A retired error-class input raises a dedicated `RemovedInputError` that subclasses **`BaseException``"
"The persistent `rebar-mcp` server must **fail startup / the write request hard** on a retired error-class input"
ABSENCE: no grace period, no escape-hatch env var, no expand-contract sequence, no documented back-out procedure is described anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `RemovedInputError(BaseException)` as a hard error from `load_config()`, deliberately bypassing all `except Exception` handlers — but provides no rollback path or back-out mechanism for operators or library consumers who cannot immediately clear a tombstoned env var (e.g., system-wide orchestration environment, a running MCP server deployment). The T4 criterion requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing, not just acknowledgement of the break.
findings.location: What section ('Distinct, un-swallowable error type') and MCP-server exit semantics section; Scope section overall
scenarios: An operator's `TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR` is set at the OS or container image level; they cannot clear it immediately. The MCP server now refuses all write requests with no override path.

A library consumer wrapping `load_config()` in `except Exception` silently stops catching config errors for tombstoned inputs — their application exits unexpectedly with an unhandled BaseException traceback (the CLI handler catches it, but library use does not go through main()).
A deployment pipeline sets `REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_RECONCILE_LIVE` globally; upgrading rebar breaks CI before the manifest can be updated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the structural `except`-handler pytest beyond `mcp_server.py` to cover all files in the config→CLI→MCP call chain (at minimum `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/config.py`, and any intermediate MCP tool wrappers), asserting no broad `except (Exception|BaseException)` block exists without a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard — or document and enforce an alternative durable invariant (e.g. a module-level `__no_broad_except__` lint rule).
evidence: The AC's structural pytest targets only `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` by name
The plan says 'Grep the config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare except: / except BaseException swallows it' — but this is a manual one-time check, not a durable test
The MCP path includes intermediate callers (CLI intercepts, tracker_dir(), LLM config load) that are not covered by the mcp_server.py-scoped structural test
ABSENCE: no AC line extends the structural grep to `_cli/__init__.py`, `config.py` tracker_dir, or any intermediate wrapper
findings.finding: The AC's structural test for broad `except` handlers that could swallow `RemovedInputError` covers only `mcp_server.py` (lines 302-312/311); the full config→CLI→MCP call chain between `load_config()` and those MCP handlers is not durably tested, so a future refactor adding a broad handler at an intermediate call site (e.g. in a CLI intercept, a `tracker_dir()` caller, or a new MCP tool wrapper) would silently re-create the exact swallowing failure the BaseException choice is meant to prevent.
findings.location: AC: 'a pytest that reads src/rebar/mcp_server.py and asserts every line matching except\s+(Exception|BaseException) has a RemovedInputError: raise guard immediately above it'
scenarios: A future developer adds a new MCP tool wrapper with `except Exception: log_and_continue()` around a config-touching call; the tombstone is silently swallowed; the bug is not caught by CI
A CLI intercept added above `_dispatch()` wraps config access in a broad except; the tombstone fires but is caught before reaching the `main()` RemovedInputError handler

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (and corresponding test) asserting that after `_resolve()` raises a `RemovedInputError` on a tombstoned TOML key, `_RESULT_CACHE` does not contain an entry for that config path/signature, verifying the cache-safety invariant is durable rather than a transient code-order property.
evidence: Plan prose: '_resolve() runs only on a cache miss and raises before populating _RESULT_CACHE'
This is a code-order invariant with no AC verifying it — no test checks that _RESULT_CACHE is empty/unchanged after a tombstone-raising _resolve() call
ABSENCE: no AC line of the form 'after a tombstoned TOML key triggers RemovedInputError, _RESULT_CACHE does not contain an entry for that config'
The plan's own TOML warm-cache argument depends entirely on this invariant holding across future _resolve() refactors
findings.finding: The plan's warm-cache-safe TOML-tombstone invariant ('_resolve() raises before populating _RESULT_CACHE, so a warm entry can only exist for a config that was clean at population time') is asserted in prose but has no corresponding AC that verifies the cache is NOT populated after a tombstone raise inside _resolve(). If a future refactor of _resolve() (e.g. partial eager caching, two-phase build) writes to _RESULT_CACHE before the tombstone check, tombstoned TOML configs silently pass on warm-cache hits — precisely the regression the design claims to prevent.
findings.location: What section: 'TOML-key tombstones … raise before populating _RESULT_CACHE, so a warm entry can only exist for a config that was clean at population time'
scenarios: A future refactor of _resolve() moves cache population to a decorator or context-manager that writes on entry (for partial caching), placing it before the tombstone check; tombstoned TOML configs silently pass on the next call
A test accidentally primes the cache before the tombstone is registered, masking the failure
findings.suggested_fix: In the table-driven test for TOML tombstones, after catching the expected RemovedInputError, inspect `config._RESULT_CACHE` (via a test-internal import or a `cache_size()` helper) and assert it is empty / does not contain the key under test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider classifying `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` tombstone as 'warn': an operator who set it intended to cap LLM step budget (now silently defaults to 250 steps), which is a cost/security-relevant behavior change — apply the same 'error' vs 'warn' heuristic used for other policy-affecting vars and document the reasoning either way.
evidence: grep for REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS returns zero live code hits
_deprecations.py:91-92 comment confirms it was 'dropped' in 'this pass, ticket 5899'
llm/config.py:604 shows REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS is the only live env var for this knob
DEFAULT_MAX_ITERATIONS raised from 50 to 250 (config.py:279), meaning an ignored REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS=20 could result in 12.5× more LLM calls than intended
findings.finding: The plan claims `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` was removed and should get a tombstone in `LLMConfig.from_env()`, classified as 'warn (LLM step budget)'. However, a full search of the codebase finds zero occurrences of `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS` anywhere (no source file, no test, no comment — except the historical note in _deprecations.py:91-92 which only mentions it by the comment 'the env alias REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS'). Since `from_env()` already reads only `REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS`, there is no existing warn/error signal path and no existing code that would need to be changed — the plan is adding a net-new tombstone check for a var that was silently dropped. The plan's classification of this tombstone as 'warn' (not 'error') contradicts its own principle: an operator who set `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS=20` to limit LLM step budget now gets unlimited steps (250), which is a behavior change affecting cost/security. The 'warn' classification may under-signal a real operational impact.
findings.location: Seed registry section: '**env, warn** ... `REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS`→`REBAR_LLM_MAX_STEPS` (LLM step budget)'
scenarios: An operator set REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS=10 in their deployment env to cap runaway LLM cost. After the removal, this is silently ignored and the default 250-step budget applies — potentially 25× cost increase.
A security-conscious operator used REBAR_LLM_MAX_ITERS to limit agent autonomy. A 'warn' tombstone lets the process continue with unbounded steps.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the 'grep the config→CLI→MCP path' audit scope to explicitly acknowledge the `except Exception` blocks in `src/rebar/llm/` that wrap `load_config()` (e.g. `plan_review/__init__.py`, `gate_dispatch.py`, `workflow_ops.py`) and confirm they do NOT need re-raise guards because `BaseException` propagates through `except Exception`.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py:76-79: `try: verify_cfg = _config.load_config(repo_root).verify / except Exception: return None`
src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:434-437: `try: return bool(_config.load_config(repo_root).verify.enable_code_review) / except Exception: return False`
Plan says 'Grep the config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare except:/except BaseException swallows it' but only names mcp_server.py:302-312/:311 for re-raise guards in Scope.
Because RemovedInputError subclasses BaseException these are NOT actual swallow risks — but unenumerated means the plan's own audit claim is incomplete.
findings.finding: The plan's 'grep the config→CLI→MCP path' audit claims coverage but only enumerates `mcp_server.py:302-312/:311` for explicit `RemovedInputError: raise` guards. Multiple `except Exception` blocks in `src/rebar/llm/` directly wrap `load_config()` calls (e.g. `llm/plan_review/__init__.py:78`, `llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:436`, `llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:335`, `llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:609`). These are outside the plan's enumerated audit scope.
findings.location: What section ('Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers') / Scope section (MCP server entry path audit)
scenarios: A code reviewer asks 'did you grep all except-Exception wrappers around load_config()?' and the answer is 'only in mcp_server.py' — leaving uncertainty about the llm/ path.
A future change widens one of these llm/ handlers to `except BaseException` without noticing the tombstone implication.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in Scope: 'Audit also covers the llm/ wrappers — `except Exception` sites do not need re-raise guards since BaseException is not Exception; only bare `except:` or `except BaseException:` blocks are at risk, and no such sites exist on the load_config() call path in llm/.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how run_workflow's background-thread config calls are covered: either (a) check tombstones synchronously in the run_workflow tool handler *before* spawning the background thread (e.g., call a `check_tombstones_or_raise()` that reads env + TOML synchronously before `threading.Thread(...).start()`), or (b) document that background-thread silencing is acceptable for this path and exclude it from the 'fail the write request hard' guarantee with a rationale.
evidence: _mcp_writes.py:306-338: `def _bg() -> None: ... except Exception as exc: # noqa: BLE001 — background run failure is reflected in run-state, not raised`
Python daemon thread behavior: unhandled exceptions in daemon threads, including BaseException subclasses, are suppressed by the thread runtime and do not crash the host process.

Plan AC: 'an in-process test that primes the config cache, sets a retired error-class input, then calls an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)' — this tests create_ticket's synchronous path, not run_workflow's async background thread.
Plan 'What' section: 'The persistent rebar-mcp server must fail startup / the write request hard on a retired error-class input rather than downgrade it to a per-request error'
findings.finding: The plan's BaseException + 'except RemovedInputError: raise' belt-and-suspenders fix is specified only for mcp_server.py:302-312/:311. However, _mcp_writes.py's run_workflow tool executes the actual config-touching work inside a daemon background thread (lines 306-338), and Python's threading.Thread silently suppresses any unhandled exception — including BaseException subclasses — in daemon threads without crashing the host process. A RemovedInputError raised from a config call within that background thread (e.g., a warm-cache env-var tombstone firing mid-process during a workflow run) would kill the daemon thread silently, leaving the run record stuck at 'running' rather than failing the write hard. The plan's in-process test for MCP ('calls an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket)') does not cover this path.
findings.location: What section — 'Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers' + AC 'MCP-server exit semantics'; _mcp_writes.py:306-338
scenarios: Operator sets TICKETS_TRACKER_DIR mid-process (or it is set from process start); warm-cache miss fires in background thread; daemon thread dies silently; run record stays 'running' forever; operator gets no migration error, no nonzero exit, no signal.
Any run_workflow invocation where env tombstone fires inside the _bg thread's config call.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a synchronous tombstone-check call (e.g., `check_active_tombstones()` that reads env vars and raises RemovedInputError) at the top of the run_workflow tool handler body, before the background thread is created. This is the same pattern as checking _allow_llm() synchronously on lines 285-295 before spawning the thread.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the explicit file/module scope of the `except (Exception|BaseException)` structural grep test (e.g. enumerate the specific call-chain files: `mcp_server.py`, `config.py`, `_cli/__init__.py`, `_mcp_writes.py`) and document that `except BaseException` cleanup-then-re-raise blocks in the store/snapshot layer are out of scope — so the test is neither too narrow nor spuriously wide.
evidence: Plan AC: 'a pytest that reads src/rebar/mcp_server.py and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a `RemovedInputError: raise` guard immediately above it (a reproducible check, not a hand grep)'
Plan What: 'Grep the config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare `except:` / `except BaseException` swallows it'

Codebase: src/rebar/_store/event_append.py, src/rebar/_store/fsutil.py, src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py, src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_planning.py all contain `except BaseException` blocks that re-raise and have no RemovedInputError guard — these are outside the config load path but would be caught by a naively widened grep
Codebase: src/rebar/llm/config.py:373 `except Exception` wraps tracker_dir() — BaseException propagates through this safely (Python language guarantee), but the path is not audited in the plan
findings.finding: The plan's AC for the MCP structural grep test — 'a pytest that reads src/rebar/mcp_server.py and asserts every line matching `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` has a RemovedInputError: raise guard immediately above it' — is stated as a reproducible, precise check, but the codebase contains `except BaseException` blocks in the broader rebar store/snapshot layer (event_append.py, fsutil.py, repo_snapshot.py, reconciler/apply_planning.py) that are legitimate cleanup-then-re-raise blocks entirely outside the config load path. The plan scopes the grep to mcp_server.py only in the AC, but the broader audit guidance in the What section says to 'grep the config→CLI→MCP path' without a defined boundary. This means the audit scope is an unverified assertion: the plan does not specify which files constitute 'the config→CLI→MCP path' for the broader grep, leaving open whether a future reader would widen the test to these re-raise blocks and produce false positives.
findings.location: What section — 'Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers' + AC bullet 'a pytest that reads src/rebar/mcp_server.py and asserts every line matching except\s+(Exception|BaseException)…'
scenarios: A future maintainer widens the structural grep test from mcp_server.py to the full rebar source tree and gets false positives from legitimate re-raise blocks, either causing spurious CI failures or prompting unnecessary RemovedInputError guards on unrelated cleanup paths.
The mcp_server.py-only scope is too narrow: a new except Exception block added to the boot path in a different file (e.g. a new mcp_server helper module) would not be caught by the file-scoped test.
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC and the What section, replace the open-ended grep guidance with an explicit module list: `[mcp_server.py, config.py, rebar/_cli/__init__.py, _mcp_writes.py]`. The test should check only those files, and the comment in the test should note that `except BaseException` in store atomic-write helpers (event_append.py, fsutil.py) is intentionally excluded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `except Exception` re-raise audit (and the AC's grep-based pytest) to cover `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py`'s `_bg()` background thread (lines 322 and 327), and explicitly decide whether `RemovedInputError` can reach that thread path and whether a re-raise guard is needed there.
evidence: src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py:322: `except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — background run failure is reflected in run-state, not raised`
src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py:327: `except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — the failure-recording path must not mask the original run error`
Plan names only mcp_server.py:302-312/:311 for the re-raise guard; _mcp_writes.py is not mentioned in the audit scope
Plan: 'verified by an in-process test … calling an MCP write-tool handler function directly (e.g. create_ticket) and asserts RemovedInputError propagates out'
findings.finding: The plan's MCP-path audit scope names only `mcp_server.py:302-312/:311` for the `except Exception` re-raise guard, but `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py` contains two nested broad `except Exception` blocks in the `_bg()` background thread (lines 322 and 327). If a `RemovedInputError` propagated into the background thread (e.g., from a config re-read triggered by an MCP write tool call during the thread's execution), it would be swallowed by the `except Exception as exc` at line 322, recording 'failed' in the run state instead of propagating — contradicting the plan's stated guarantee that the MCP write path 'fail[s] startup / the write request hard'.
findings.location: What section — 'Defensively re-raise at the MCP boot handlers … Grep the config→CLI→MCP path to confirm no bare except: / except BaseException swallows it'; also Scope: mcp_server.py:302-312/:311
scenarios: A RemovedInputError raised inside the _bg() background thread of run_workflow is caught by the except Exception at _mcp_writes.py:322, silently recorded as a 'failed' run status instead of propagating the hard migration error.
The AC's pytest that checks `except\s+(Exception|BaseException)` lines for a re-raise guard above them would miss _mcp_writes.py if the grep pattern is only applied to mcp_server.py.

findings.finding: The plan refers to `mcp_server.py:302-312` and `mcp_server.py:311` as two separate `except Exception` blocks requiring `except RemovedInputError: raise` guards, but inspecting the file shows these are one single block (line 311 is inside the 302-312 block). There is only one `except Exception` in `mcp_server.py`. Treating them as two distinct locations will confuse the implementer and may lead to a double-guard on the same block.
findings.location: What section / Implementation sequencing (Slice 1) / `src/rebar/mcp_server.py:302-312`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`mcp_server.py:302-312`/`:311`' with '`mcp_server.py:302-312` (a single block)' throughout the plan.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
